items.title:
2600 BCE: Indus Valley Seals
One-horned beasts appear on Indus Valley civilization seals,
representing what archaeologists diplomatically call 'profile-view bulls.'
The unicorn interpretation persists because it is more fundable.

400 BCE: Ctesias writes 'Indica'
Greek physician Ctesias describes a wild donkey with a single horn,
based on secondhand accounts from Persia. Peer review was not yet standard practice.

200 CE: Chinese Qilin Traditions
The Qilin, a chimerical beast sometimes depicted with a single horn,
is codified in Chinese literary traditions as an omen of prosperity.
Its appearance reportedly coincides with the arrival of sage rulers,
a correlation that has not held up to modern statistical analysis.

1298: Marco Polo Reports Unicorns
Marco Polo encounters rhinoceroses in Sumatra and identifies them as unicorns,
noting they are 'very ugly brutes to look at.' This is considered
the earliest known example of reality failing to match the pitch deck.

1503: Leonardo da Vinci's Drawing
Leonardo da Vinci creates sketches of a lady with a unicorn,
establishing the visual brand identity that would endure for five centuries.
The unicorn's marketing team has been remarkably consistent.

1560: Peak Unicorn Horn Trade
Narwhal tusks sold as 'unicorn horns' command prices exceeding
their weight in gold across European courts. The due diligence
process consisted primarily of wanting it to be true.

1825: Baron Cuvier's Declaration
French zoologist Baron Cuvier declares unicorns anatomically impossible,
citing the cloven hoof and divided frontal bone. The market
briefly corrects before resuming its previous trajectory.

1933: One-Horned Horse in Maine
A shire horse born with a single central horn in Maine
briefly reignites unicorn speculation. Scientists attribute it
to a developmental anomaly. The horse declines to comment.

2013: The Unicorn Metaphor Is Born
Venture capitalist Aileen Lee coins 'unicorn' to describe
startups valued at $1 billion or more, on the grounds
that they are statistically rare. The metaphor ages poorly
as the count exceeds 1,000 within a decade.

2024: 1,200+ Unicorn Startups
The global unicorn count surpasses 1,200 with a combined valuation
of $4.7 trillion. The term 'unicorn,' chosen for its rarity,
now describes something more common than profitable companies.
The irony remains unacknowledged in investor presentations.
